chunk_summary: This section outlines preoperative and postoperative nutrition strategies for congenital heart disease (CHD) surgery, emphasizing calorie and protein targets, weight monitoring, and staged calorie progression to optimize surgical readiness and recovery.
learning_objectives: Understand the importance of time-limited, metric-driven nutrition optimization before CHD surgery.
Learn calorie and protein targets for high-risk CHD physiologies and postoperative recovery.
Identify strategies to manage common postoperative interruptions and ensure adequate nutrition delivery.
Define surgical readiness and recovery metrics for CHD patients.
key_facts: Preoperative nutrition optimization should be time-limited to 1–2 weeks, with explicit calorie (130–150 kcal/kg/day) and protein targets.
High-risk CHD physiologies, such as single ventricle, often require caloric densities of 24–27 kcal/oz.
Postoperative calorie delivery starts at 55–60 kcal/kg/day and progresses to 90–100 kcal/kg/day as sedation and ventilation decrease.
Protein provision is critical postoperatively to preserve lean mass and support wound healing.
Surgical readiness is defined by delivering ≥90% of prescribed calories, stable respiratory status during feeds, and improving growth metrics.
reference_quote: Perioperative nutrition in pediatric patients with congenital heart disease requires explicit calorie and protein targets, with staged calorie progression postoperatively. (Silva-Gburek et al.)
document_key_topics: Preoperative nutrition optimization for CHD surgery
